primary_diagnosis.name: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I) of the right lower limb — acute exacerbation

differential.evidence_basis: Burning pain vastly out of proportion to a recent minor fracture, mechanical allodynia (bedsheet intolerable), unilateral swelling and shiny/mottled skin — the post-traumatic CRPS constellation.
Acute unilateral leg swelling after a limb injury/immobilisation — DVT must be actively excluded; telehealth cannot palpate the calf or check pulses.
Unilateral erythema and swelling — but pain is out of proportion to the erythema and there is no fever.
Severe pain in a recently fractured limb — must be considered, though the sub-acute weeks-long course and lack of a fresh high-energy injury argue against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even before questioning, DVT and compartment syndrome must appear as important_not_to_miss because telehealth cannot examine the limb — but a burning, allodynic, out-of-proportion pain after a minor fracture should already point to CRPS as leading rather than triggering reflexive emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Mechanical allodynia + vasomotor/sudomotor change + oedema + trophic skin change + pain out of proportion, all after a fracture — meets the Budapest clinical pattern. Afebrile, foot warm/pink/sensate with no spreading erythema.
Afebrile, no spreading erythema or streaking, pain grossly out of proportion to the redness — clinically argues strongly against cellulitis.

Superficial mechanical allodynia and vasomotor change are not features of DVT, and there are no PE symptoms — but DVT cannot be definitively excluded remotely without a Doppler, so it stays on the list for safety-netting.
Weeks-long sub-acute course rather than a tense acutely-injured compartment makes this unlikely, but severe pain warrants explicit safety-netting for it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the picture is a benign-but-severe chronic CRPS flare with a NEGATIVE acute-red-flag screen. The correct move is to recognise CRPS, manage at T2, and safety-net the mimics — NOT to escalate the whole presentation to ED because the limb cannot be palpated remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Post-fracture complex regional pain syndrome, acute exacerbation — a known chronic entity managed longitudinally, presenting in a painful flare. Presumptive on telehealth; GP review + multidisciplinary pain care is the correct pathway.

Retained as a safety-net item — a venous Doppler and in-person limb assessment at GP review formally exclude it; the patient is netted to escalate if calf pain/swelling worsens or PE features develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final answer is a presumptive CRPS-I flare managed at T2 with prompt GP review, multidisciplinary pain referral, and explicit escalation safety-netting. Confident emergency escalation (T4/T5 baseline) is over-triage; confident sole CRPS diagnosis with NO DVT safety-net is unsafe under-netting.

red_flags.description: Limb becomes pale, cold, mottled-dusky, numb, or pulseless — acute limb ischaemia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal a threatened limb requiring emergency vascular assessment. Currently absent — the patient reports the foot is warm, normal-coloured and sensate — but must be screened and safety-netted because telehealth cannot confirm pulses.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing acute limb ischaemia is not recognised; irreversible limb loss within hours.

red_flags.description: New high fever, rigors, or spreading erythema / red streaking / systemic unwellness — cellulitis with lymphangitis or sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would convert the picture to a serious soft-tissue infection needing urgent assessment and IV antibiotics. Currently absent (afebrile, no spreading redness), but a low threshold to re-present is required.

red_flags.description: Worsening unilateral calf pain/swelling, or new pleuritic chest pain / breathlessness — deep vein thrombosis with possible pulmonary embolism
red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT cannot be excluded via telehealth (no calf palpation, no Doppler). The unilateral swelling is currently attributable to the CRPS flare and PE screen is negative, but the mimic must be safety-netted and formally excluded at in-person review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated DVT propagates or embolises to a fatal PE.

red_flags.description: Rapidly increasing tense swelling with severe pain on passive movement of the toes, or new sensory/motor loss — acute compartment syndrome
red_flags.clinical_significance: A surgical emergency if it develops in the recently fractured limb. Sub-acute course and soft compartments on the source in-person exam make it currently very unlikely, but it must be netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed compartment syndrome causes muscle necrosis and permanent contracture.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled pain crisis in a person living alone and unable to weight-bear — high fall/harm risk at home
red_flags.clinical_significance: Not a medical emergency in itself, but the combination of a severe uncontrolled pain crisis, bedbound state, social isolation and a cluttered environment materially raises the priority of the GP review and warrants a home-safety plan.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A fall with injury at home with no one to summon help.

pathophysiology_summary: Complex regional pain syndrome type I is a post-traumatic neuro-inflammatory pain disorder in which, after a triggering injury (here a minor tibial fracture), aberrant peripheral and central sensitisation combine with dysregulated sympathetic/autonomic outflow to produce pain grossly out of proportion to the inciting event, mechanical allodynia, and vasomotor (colour/temperature), sudomotor (sweating), oedematous and trophic (shiny/tight skin) changes in the affected limb. Diagnosis is CLINICAL (Budapest Criteria) and is one of exclusion — the alarming acute mimics (DVT, cellulitis, acute compartment syndrome, acute limb ischaemia) must be considered. In this case the source clinician excluded DVT/compartment/ischaemia on in-person examination (palpable bilateral pulses, soft compartments, no calf tenderness, Homan's negative) and cellulitis clinically (afebrile, pain out of proportion to erythema). The telehealth AI Doctor cannot palpate or check pulses, so it recognises the characteristic CRPS flare on the patient-obtainable data, manages the pain crisis, arranges prompt GP review with an in-person limb exam +/- venous Doppler, and safety-nets the mimics prominently — without reflexively over-escalating a stable-vital, afebrile, sensate, warm-limbed chronic flare to emergency care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively escalating the whole presentation to ED or 000 because the limb cannot be examined remotely — over-triaging a stable, afebrile, sensate, warm-limbed chronic CRPS flare with a negative acute-red-flag screen is the primary scored defect for this case.
Being swept up by the dramatic language ('on fire', 'absolute agony', 'exploded') and the source's own 'urgent evaluation' framing into treating severity of distress as severity of acute danger.
Confidently diagnosing CRPS and failing to safety-net DVT — telehealth cannot exclude DVT without calf examination and Doppler, so a DVT/PE escalation edge is mandatory.
Anchoring on DVT or compartment syndrome and missing that the pain-out-of-proportion + allodynia + vasomotor cluster is far more consistent with a CRPS flare of a known post-fracture problem.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of distal pulses (dorsalis pedis / posterior tibial) to exclude acute limb ischaemia
Calf palpation, tenderness and Homan's assessment for DVT
Compartment softness/tension on deep palpation for compartment syndrome
Objective examination-grade elicitation of allodynia and true limb temperature/colour
Venous Doppler ultrasound to formally exclude DVT
Formal vital-sign measurement (only patient-reported/home-device readings are available)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the likely CRPS-I flare of the known post-fracture injury and manage at T2: arrange prompt in-person GP review (within 2-3 days) for limb examination +/- venous Doppler, start the neuropathic-pain plan, address the home-safety/fall risk, and give explicit, prominent safety-netting for the mimics (limb going pale/cold/numb, fever/spreading redness, worsening calf swelling or breathlessness) — do NOT baseline-escalate to ED/000.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: over-diagnosing an emergency (sending a stable chronic flare to ED/000) wastes resources and causes harm/anxiety and is the main scored defect; but confidently diagnosing CRPS with NO DVT/ischaemia safety-net risks a missed DVT/PE or threatened limb.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Budapest Criteria (Harden RN et al., Pain 2010) — clinical diagnostic criteria for CRPS
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: CRPS is a clinical diagnosis requiring continuing pain disproportionate to the inciting event plus signs/symptoms across sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor/oedema, and motor/trophic categories, with no better explanation — mandating exclusion of mimics.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Pain and Analgesia — neuropathic pain (current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Neuropathic/CRPS pain is managed with a multimodal plan including a tricyclic antidepressant (e.g. low-dose amitriptyline) and multidisciplinary rehabilitation (graded motor imagery/mirror therapy, physiotherapy); simple analgesics are frequently ineffective.
